instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by dereferencing null pointer.

input.title: Dereferencing Null Pointer

output.technical: A segmentation fault occurs when a program attempts to access a memory location that it is not allowed to access. Dereferencing a `nullptr` attempts to read from memory address 0x0, which is protected by the operating system.
output.beginner: You tried to look inside a box (pointer), but the box didn't exist (it was `nullptr`). The computer panicked and crashed.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by std::out_of_range exception.

input.title: std::out_of_range Exception
input.error_message:
terminate called after throwing an instance of 'std::out_of_range'
  what():  vector::_M_range_check: __n (which is 5) >= this->size() (which is 3)
Aborted (core dumped)

output.technical: The `std::vector::at()` method performs bounds checking. If the requested index is greater than or equal to the vector's size, it throws a `std::out_of_range` exception, which terminates the program if unhandled.
output.beginner: You asked for the 6th item in a list that only has 3 items. C++ caught the mistake and threw an exception to stop you from reading random memory.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by integer division by zero.

input.title: Integer Division by Zero

output.technical: Attempting to perform integer division by zero raises a SIGFPE (Floating-Point Exception) signal in POSIX systems, which typically terminates the process immediately.
output.beginner: You tried to divide a number by zero. Just like in math class, this is impossible, so the computer crashes.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by stack overflow (infinite recursion).

input.title: Stack Overflow (Infinite Recursion)

output.technical: When a function calls itself recursively without a reachable base case, it continuously adds new stack frames to the call stack. Eventually, the stack memory is exhausted, causing a stack overflow (usually manifesting as a segmentation fault).
output.beginner: You told a function to call itself forever without a stopping condition. Eventually, the computer ran out of memory to keep track of the calls, and crashed.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by std::bad_alloc exception.

input.title: std::bad_alloc Exception

output.technical: When the `new` operator fails to allocate the requested amount of contiguous heap memory (either because the system is out of memory or the requested size is absurd), it throws a `std::bad_alloc` exception.
output.beginner: You asked the computer for way more memory than it actually has. It couldn't fulfill the request, so it threw an error.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by stack overflow (infinite recursion).

input.title: Stack Overflow (Infinite Recursion)

output.technical: When a function calls itself recursively without a reachable base case, it continuously adds new stack frames to the call stack. Eventually, the stack memory is exhausted, causing a stack overflow (usually manifesting as a segmentation fault).
output.beginner: You told a function to call itself forever without a stopping condition. Eventually, the computer ran out of memory to keep track of the calls, and crashed.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by std::bad_alloc exception.

input.title: std::bad_alloc Exception

output.technical: When the `new` operator fails to allocate the requested amount of contiguous heap memory (either because the system is out of memory or the requested size is absurd), it throws a `std::bad_alloc` exception.
output.beginner: You asked the computer for way more memory than it actually has. It couldn't fulfill the request, so it threw an error.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by dereferencing null pointer.

input.title: Dereferencing Null Pointer

output.technical: A segmentation fault occurs when a program attempts to access a memory location that it is not allowed to access. Dereferencing a `nullptr` attempts to read from memory address 0x0, which is protected by the operating system.
output.beginner: You tried to look inside a box (pointer), but the box didn't exist (it was `nullptr`). The computer panicked and crashed.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by std::out_of_range exception.

input.title: std::out_of_range Exception
input.error_message:
terminate called after throwing an instance of 'std::out_of_range'
  what():  vector::_M_range_check: __n (which is 5) >= this->size() (which is 3)
Aborted (core dumped)

output.technical: The `std::vector::at()` method performs bounds checking. If the requested index is greater than or equal to the vector's size, it throws a `std::out_of_range` exception, which terminates the program if unhandled.
output.beginner: You asked for the 6th item in a list that only has 3 items. C++ caught the mistake and threw an exception to stop you from reading random memory.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by std::bad_alloc exception.

input.title: std::bad_alloc Exception

output.technical: When the `new` operator fails to allocate the requested amount of contiguous heap memory (either because the system is out of memory or the requested size is absurd), it throws a `std::bad_alloc` exception.
output.beginner: You asked the computer for way more memory than it actually has. It couldn't fulfill the request, so it threw an error.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by dereferencing null pointer.

input.title: Dereferencing Null Pointer

output.technical: A segmentation fault occurs when a program attempts to access a memory location that it is not allowed to access. Dereferencing a `nullptr` attempts to read from memory address 0x0, which is protected by the operating system.
output.beginner: You tried to look inside a box (pointer), but the box didn't exist (it was `nullptr`). The computer panicked and crashed.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by std::out_of_range exception.

input.title: std::out_of_range Exception
input.error_message:
terminate called after throwing an instance of 'std::out_of_range'
  what():  vector::_M_range_check: __n (which is 5) >= this->size() (which is 3)
Aborted (core dumped)

output.technical: The `std::vector::at()` method performs bounds checking. If the requested index is greater than or equal to the vector's size, it throws a `std::out_of_range` exception, which terminates the program if unhandled.
output.beginner: You asked for the 6th item in a list that only has 3 items. C++ caught the mistake and threw an exception to stop you from reading random memory.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by stack overflow (infinite recursion).

input.title: Stack Overflow (Infinite Recursion)

output.technical: When a function calls itself recursively without a reachable base case, it continuously adds new stack frames to the call stack. Eventually, the stack memory is exhausted, causing a stack overflow (usually manifesting as a segmentation fault).
output.beginner: You told a function to call itself forever without a stopping condition. Eventually, the computer ran out of memory to keep track of the calls, and crashed.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by std::out_of_range exception.

input.title: std::out_of_range Exception
input.error_message:
terminate called after throwing an instance of 'std::out_of_range'
  what():  vector::_M_range_check: __n (which is 5) >= this->size() (which is 3)
Aborted (core dumped)

output.technical: The `std::vector::at()` method performs bounds checking. If the requested index is greater than or equal to the vector's size, it throws a `std::out_of_range` exception, which terminates the program if unhandled.
output.beginner: You asked for the 6th item in a list that only has 3 items. C++ caught the mistake and threw an exception to stop you from reading random memory.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by integer division by zero.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by stack overflow (infinite recursion).

input.title: Stack Overflow (Infinite Recursion)

output.technical: When a function calls itself recursively without a reachable base case, it continuously adds new stack frames to the call stack. Eventually, the stack memory is exhausted, causing a stack overflow (usually manifesting as a segmentation fault).
output.beginner: You told a function to call itself forever without a stopping condition. Eventually, the computer ran out of memory to keep track of the calls, and crashed.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by std::bad_alloc exception.

input.title: std::bad_alloc Exception

output.technical: When the `new` operator fails to allocate the requested amount of contiguous heap memory (either because the system is out of memory or the requested size is absurd), it throws a `std::bad_alloc` exception.
output.beginner: You asked the computer for way more memory than it actually has. It couldn't fulfill the request, so it threw an error.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by dereferencing null pointer.

input.title: Dereferencing Null Pointer

output.technical: A segmentation fault occurs when a program attempts to access a memory location that it is not allowed to access. Dereferencing a `nullptr` attempts to read from memory address 0x0, which is protected by the operating system.
output.beginner: You tried to look inside a box (pointer), but the box didn't exist (it was `nullptr`). The computer panicked and crashed.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

output.beginner: You tried to divide a number by zero. Just like in math class, this is impossible, so the computer crashes.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by stack overflow (infinite recursion).

input.title: Stack Overflow (Infinite Recursion)

output.technical: When a function calls itself recursively without a reachable base case, it continuously adds new stack frames to the call stack. Eventually, the stack memory is exhausted, causing a stack overflow (usually manifesting as a segmentation fault).
output.beginner: You told a function to call itself forever without a stopping condition. Eventually, the computer ran out of memory to keep track of the calls, and crashed.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by std::bad_alloc exception.

input.title: std::bad_alloc Exception

output.technical: When the `new` operator fails to allocate the requested amount of contiguous heap memory (either because the system is out of memory or the requested size is absurd), it throws a `std::bad_alloc` exception.
output.beginner: You asked the computer for way more memory than it actually has. It couldn't fulfill the request, so it threw an error.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by dereferencing null pointer.

input.title: Dereferencing Null Pointer

output.technical: A segmentation fault occurs when a program attempts to access a memory location that it is not allowed to access. Dereferencing a `nullptr` attempts to read from memory address 0x0, which is protected by the operating system.
output.beginner: You tried to look inside a box (pointer), but the box didn't exist (it was `nullptr`). The computer panicked and crashed.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by std::out_of_range exception.

input.title: std::out_of_range Exception
input.error_message:
terminate called after throwing an instance of 'std::out_of_range'
  what():  vector::_M_range_check: __n (which is 5) >= this->size() (which is 3)
Aborted (core dumped)

output.technical: The `std::vector::at()` method performs bounds checking. If the requested index is greater than or equal to the vector's size, it throws a `std::out_of_range` exception, which terminates the program if unhandled.
output.beginner: You asked for the 6th item in a list that only has 3 items. C++ caught the mistake and threw an exception to stop you from reading random memory.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

output.beginner: You asked the computer for way more memory than it actually has. It couldn't fulfill the request, so it threw an error.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by dereferencing null pointer.

input.title: Dereferencing Null Pointer

output.technical: A segmentation fault occurs when a program attempts to access a memory location that it is not allowed to access. Dereferencing a `nullptr` attempts to read from memory address 0x0, which is protected by the operating system.
output.beginner: You tried to look inside a box (pointer), but the box didn't exist (it was `nullptr`). The computer panicked and crashed.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by std::out_of_range exception.

input.title: std::out_of_range Exception
input.error_message:
terminate called after throwing an instance of 'std::out_of_range'
  what():  vector::_M_range_check: __n (which is 5) >= this->size() (which is 3)
Aborted (core dumped)

output.technical: The `std::vector::at()` method performs bounds checking. If the requested index is greater than or equal to the vector's size, it throws a `std::out_of_range` exception, which terminates the program if unhandled.
output.beginner: You asked for the 6th item in a list that only has 3 items. C++ caught the mistake and threw an exception to stop you from reading random memory.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by integer division by zero.

input.title: Integer Division by Zero

output.technical: Attempting to perform integer division by zero raises a SIGFPE (Floating-Point Exception) signal in POSIX systems, which typically terminates the process immediately.
output.beginner: You tried to divide a number by zero. Just like in math class, this is impossible, so the computer crashes.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by stack overflow (infinite recursion).

input.title: Stack Overflow (Infinite Recursion)

output.technical: When a function calls itself recursively without a reachable base case, it continuously adds new stack frames to the call stack. Eventually, the stack memory is exhausted, causing a stack overflow (usually manifesting as a segmentation fault).
output.beginner: You told a function to call itself forever without a stopping condition. Eventually, the computer ran out of memory to keep track of the calls, and crashed.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

  what():  vector::_M_range_check: __n (which is 5) >= this->size() (which is 3)
Aborted (core dumped)

output.technical: The `std::vector::at()` method performs bounds checking. If the requested index is greater than or equal to the vector's size, it throws a `std::out_of_range` exception, which terminates the program if unhandled.
output.beginner: You asked for the 6th item in a list that only has 3 items. C++ caught the mistake and threw an exception to stop you from reading random memory.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by integer division by zero.

input.title: Integer Division by Zero

output.technical: Attempting to perform integer division by zero raises a SIGFPE (Floating-Point Exception) signal in POSIX systems, which typically terminates the process immediately.
output.beginner: You tried to divide a number by zero. Just like in math class, this is impossible, so the computer crashes.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by stack overflow (infinite recursion).

input.title: Stack Overflow (Infinite Recursion)

output.technical: When a function calls itself recursively without a reachable base case, it continuously adds new stack frames to the call stack. Eventually, the stack memory is exhausted, causing a stack overflow (usually manifesting as a segmentation fault).
output.beginner: You told a function to call itself forever without a stopping condition. Eventually, the computer ran out of memory to keep track of the calls, and crashed.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by std::bad_alloc exception.

input.title: std::bad_alloc Exception

output.technical: When the `new` operator fails to allocate the requested amount of contiguous heap memory (either because the system is out of memory or the requested size is absurd), it throws a `std::bad_alloc` exception.
output.beginner: You asked the computer for way more memory than it actually has. It couldn't fulfill the request, so it threw an error.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by dereferencing null pointer.

input.title: Dereferencing Null Pointer

output.technical: A segmentation fault occurs when a program attempts to access a memory location that it is not allowed to access. Dereferencing a `nullptr` attempts to read from memory address 0x0, which is protected by the operating system.
output.beginner: You tried to look inside a box (pointer), but the box didn't exist (it was `nullptr`). The computer panicked and crashed.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

input.title: Stack Overflow (Infinite Recursion)

output.technical: When a function calls itself recursively without a reachable base case, it continuously adds new stack frames to the call stack. Eventually, the stack memory is exhausted, causing a stack overflow (usually manifesting as a segmentation fault).
output.beginner: You told a function to call itself forever without a stopping condition. Eventually, the computer ran out of memory to keep track of the calls, and crashed.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by std::bad_alloc exception.

input.title: std::bad_alloc Exception

output.technical: When the `new` operator fails to allocate the requested amount of contiguous heap memory (either because the system is out of memory or the requested size is absurd), it throws a `std::bad_alloc` exception.
output.beginner: You asked the computer for way more memory than it actually has. It couldn't fulfill the request, so it threw an error.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by dereferencing null pointer.

input.title: Dereferencing Null Pointer

output.technical: A segmentation fault occurs when a program attempts to access a memory location that it is not allowed to access. Dereferencing a `nullptr` attempts to read from memory address 0x0, which is protected by the operating system.
output.beginner: You tried to look inside a box (pointer), but the box didn't exist (it was `nullptr`). The computer panicked and crashed.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by std::out_of_range exception.

input.title: std::out_of_range Exception
input.error_message:
terminate called after throwing an instance of 'std::out_of_range'
  what():  vector::_M_range_check: __n (which is 5) >= this->size() (which is 3)
Aborted (core dumped)

output.technical: The `std::vector::at()` method performs bounds checking. If the requested index is greater than or equal to the vector's size, it throws a `std::out_of_range` exception, which terminates the program if unhandled.
output.beginner: You asked for the 6th item in a list that only has 3 items. C++ caught the mistake and threw an exception to stop you from reading random memory.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

input.title: Dereferencing Null Pointer

output.technical: A segmentation fault occurs when a program attempts to access a memory location that it is not allowed to access. Dereferencing a `nullptr` attempts to read from memory address 0x0, which is protected by the operating system.
output.beginner: You tried to look inside a box (pointer), but the box didn't exist (it was `nullptr`). The computer panicked and crashed.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by std::out_of_range exception.

input.title: std::out_of_range Exception
input.error_message:
terminate called after throwing an instance of 'std::out_of_range'
  what():  vector::_M_range_check: __n (which is 5) >= this->size() (which is 3)
Aborted (core dumped)

output.technical: The `std::vector::at()` method performs bounds checking. If the requested index is greater than or equal to the vector's size, it throws a `std::out_of_range` exception, which terminates the program if unhandled.
output.beginner: You asked for the 6th item in a list that only has 3 items. C++ caught the mistake and threw an exception to stop you from reading random memory.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by integer division by zero.

input.title: Integer Division by Zero

output.technical: Attempting to perform integer division by zero raises a SIGFPE (Floating-Point Exception) signal in POSIX systems, which typically terminates the process immediately.
output.beginner: You tried to divide a number by zero. Just like in math class, this is impossible, so the computer crashes.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by stack overflow (infinite recursion).

input.title: Stack Overflow (Infinite Recursion)

output.technical: When a function calls itself recursively without a reachable base case, it continuously adds new stack frames to the call stack. Eventually, the stack memory is exhausted, causing a stack overflow (usually manifesting as a segmentation fault).
output.beginner: You told a function to call itself forever without a stopping condition. Eventually, the computer ran out of memory to keep track of the calls, and crashed.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by std::bad_alloc exception.

input.title: std::bad_alloc Exception

output.technical: When the `new` operator fails to allocate the requested amount of contiguous heap memory (either because the system is out of memory or the requested size is absurd), it throws a `std::bad_alloc` exception.
output.beginner: You asked the computer for way more memory than it actually has. It couldn't fulfill the request, so it threw an error.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by stack overflow (infinite recursion).

input.title: Stack Overflow (Infinite Recursion)

output.technical: When a function calls itself recursively without a reachable base case, it continuously adds new stack frames to the call stack. Eventually, the stack memory is exhausted, causing a stack overflow (usually manifesting as a segmentation fault).
output.beginner: You told a function to call itself forever without a stopping condition. Eventually, the computer ran out of memory to keep track of the calls, and crashed.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by std::bad_alloc exception.

input.title: std::bad_alloc Exception

output.technical: When the `new` operator fails to allocate the requested amount of contiguous heap memory (either because the system is out of memory or the requested size is absurd), it throws a `std::bad_alloc` exception.
output.beginner: You asked the computer for way more memory than it actually has. It couldn't fulfill the request, so it threw an error.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by dereferencing null pointer.

input.title: Dereferencing Null Pointer

output.technical: A segmentation fault occurs when a program attempts to access a memory location that it is not allowed to access. Dereferencing a `nullptr` attempts to read from memory address 0x0, which is protected by the operating system.
output.beginner: You tried to look inside a box (pointer), but the box didn't exist (it was `nullptr`). The computer panicked and crashed.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by std::out_of_range exception.

input.title: std::out_of_range Exception
input.error_message:
terminate called after throwing an instance of 'std::out_of_range'
  what():  vector::_M_range_check: __n (which is 5) >= this->size() (which is 3)
Aborted (core dumped)

output.technical: The `std::vector::at()` method performs bounds checking. If the requested index is greater than or equal to the vector's size, it throws a `std::out_of_range` exception, which terminates the program if unhandled.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by std::bad_alloc exception.

input.title: std::bad_alloc Exception

output.technical: When the `new` operator fails to allocate the requested amount of contiguous heap memory (either because the system is out of memory or the requested size is absurd), it throws a `std::bad_alloc` exception.
output.beginner: You asked the computer for way more memory than it actually has. It couldn't fulfill the request, so it threw an error.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by dereferencing null pointer.

input.title: Dereferencing Null Pointer

output.technical: A segmentation fault occurs when a program attempts to access a memory location that it is not allowed to access. Dereferencing a `nullptr` attempts to read from memory address 0x0, which is protected by the operating system.
output.beginner: You tried to look inside a box (pointer), but the box didn't exist (it was `nullptr`). The computer panicked and crashed.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by std::out_of_range exception.

input.title: std::out_of_range Exception
input.error_message:
terminate called after throwing an instance of 'std::out_of_range'
  what():  vector::_M_range_check: __n (which is 5) >= this->size() (which is 3)
Aborted (core dumped)

output.technical: The `std::vector::at()` method performs bounds checking. If the requested index is greater than or equal to the vector's size, it throws a `std::out_of_range` exception, which terminates the program if unhandled.
output.beginner: You asked for the 6th item in a list that only has 3 items. C++ caught the mistake and threw an exception to stop you from reading random memory.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by std::out_of_range exception.

input.title: std::out_of_range Exception
input.error_message:
terminate called after throwing an instance of 'std::out_of_range'
  what():  vector::_M_range_check: __n (which is 5) >= this->size() (which is 3)
Aborted (core dumped)

output.technical: The `std::vector::at()` method performs bounds checking. If the requested index is greater than or equal to the vector's size, it throws a `std::out_of_range` exception, which terminates the program if unhandled.
output.beginner: You asked for the 6th item in a list that only has 3 items. C++ caught the mistake and threw an exception to stop you from reading random memory.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by stack overflow (infinite recursion).

input.title: Stack Overflow (Infinite Recursion)

output.technical: When a function calls itself recursively without a reachable base case, it continuously adds new stack frames to the call stack. Eventually, the stack memory is exhausted, causing a stack overflow (usually manifesting as a segmentation fault).
output.beginner: You told a function to call itself forever without a stopping condition. Eventually, the computer ran out of memory to keep track of the calls, and crashed.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by std::bad_alloc exception.

input.title: std::bad_alloc Exception

output.technical: When the `new` operator fails to allocate the requested amount of contiguous heap memory (either because the system is out of memory or the requested size is absurd), it throws a `std::bad_alloc` exception.
output.beginner: You asked the computer for way more memory than it actually has. It couldn't fulfill the request, so it threw an error.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

output.technical: Attempting to perform integer division by zero raises a SIGFPE (Floating-Point Exception) signal in POSIX systems, which typically terminates the process immediately.
output.beginner: You tried to divide a number by zero. Just like in math class, this is impossible, so the computer crashes.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by stack overflow (infinite recursion).

input.title: Stack Overflow (Infinite Recursion)

output.technical: When a function calls itself recursively without a reachable base case, it continuously adds new stack frames to the call stack. Eventually, the stack memory is exhausted, causing a stack overflow (usually manifesting as a segmentation fault).
output.beginner: You told a function to call itself forever without a stopping condition. Eventually, the computer ran out of memory to keep track of the calls, and crashed.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by std::bad_alloc exception.

input.title: std::bad_alloc Exception

output.technical: When the `new` operator fails to allocate the requested amount of contiguous heap memory (either because the system is out of memory or the requested size is absurd), it throws a `std::bad_alloc` exception.
output.beginner: You asked the computer for way more memory than it actually has. It couldn't fulfill the request, so it threw an error.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by dereferencing null pointer.

input.title: Dereferencing Null Pointer

output.technical: A segmentation fault occurs when a program attempts to access a memory location that it is not allowed to access. Dereferencing a `nullptr` attempts to read from memory address 0x0, which is protected by the operating system.
output.beginner: You tried to look inside a box (pointer), but the box didn't exist (it was `nullptr`). The computer panicked and crashed.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by std::out_of_range exception.

input.title: std::out_of_range Exception
input.error_message:
terminate called after throwing an instance of 'std::out_of_range'
  what():  vector::_M_range_check: __n (which is 5) >= this->size() (which is 3)
Aborted (core dumped)

output.technical: The `std::vector::at()` method performs bounds checking. If the requested index is greater than or equal to the vector's size, it throws a `std::out_of_range` exception, which terminates the program if unhandled.
output.beginner: You asked for the 6th item in a list that only has 3 items. C++ caught the mistake and threw an exception to stop you from reading random memory.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

output.technical: When the `new` operator fails to allocate the requested amount of contiguous heap memory (either because the system is out of memory or the requested size is absurd), it throws a `std::bad_alloc` exception.
output.beginner: You asked the computer for way more memory than it actually has. It couldn't fulfill the request, so it threw an error.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by dereferencing null pointer.

input.title: Dereferencing Null Pointer

output.technical: A segmentation fault occurs when a program attempts to access a memory location that it is not allowed to access. Dereferencing a `nullptr` attempts to read from memory address 0x0, which is protected by the operating system.
output.beginner: You tried to look inside a box (pointer), but the box didn't exist (it was `nullptr`). The computer panicked and crashed.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by std::out_of_range exception.

input.title: std::out_of_range Exception
input.error_message:
terminate called after throwing an instance of 'std::out_of_range'
  what():  vector::_M_range_check: __n (which is 5) >= this->size() (which is 3)
Aborted (core dumped)

output.technical: The `std::vector::at()` method performs bounds checking. If the requested index is greater than or equal to the vector's size, it throws a `std::out_of_range` exception, which terminates the program if unhandled.
output.beginner: You asked for the 6th item in a list that only has 3 items. C++ caught the mistake and threw an exception to stop you from reading random memory.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by integer division by zero.

input.title: Integer Division by Zero

output.technical: Attempting to perform integer division by zero raises a SIGFPE (Floating-Point Exception) signal in POSIX systems, which typically terminates the process immediately.
output.beginner: You tried to divide a number by zero. Just like in math class, this is impossible, so the computer crashes.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by stack overflow (infinite recursion).

input.title: Stack Overflow (Infinite Recursion)

output.technical: When a function calls itself recursively without a reachable base case, it continuously adds new stack frames to the call stack. Eventually, the stack memory is exhausted, causing a stack overflow (usually manifesting as a segmentation fault).
output.beginner: You told a function to call itself forever without a stopping condition. Eventually, the computer ran out of memory to keep track of the calls, and crashed.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

  what():  vector::_M_range_check: __n (which is 5) >= this->size() (which is 3)
Aborted (core dumped)

output.technical: The `std::vector::at()` method performs bounds checking. If the requested index is greater than or equal to the vector's size, it throws a `std::out_of_range` exception, which terminates the program if unhandled.
output.beginner: You asked for the 6th item in a list that only has 3 items. C++ caught the mistake and threw an exception to stop you from reading random memory.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by integer division by zero.

input.title: Integer Division by Zero

output.technical: Attempting to perform integer division by zero raises a SIGFPE (Floating-Point Exception) signal in POSIX systems, which typically terminates the process immediately.
output.beginner: You tried to divide a number by zero. Just like in math class, this is impossible, so the computer crashes.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by stack overflow (infinite recursion).

input.title: Stack Overflow (Infinite Recursion)

output.technical: When a function calls itself recursively without a reachable base case, it continuously adds new stack frames to the call stack. Eventually, the stack memory is exhausted, causing a stack overflow (usually manifesting as a segmentation fault).
output.beginner: You told a function to call itself forever without a stopping condition. Eventually, the computer ran out of memory to keep track of the calls, and crashed.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by std::bad_alloc exception.

input.title: std::bad_alloc Exception

output.technical: When the `new` operator fails to allocate the requested amount of contiguous heap memory (either because the system is out of memory or the requested size is absurd), it throws a `std::bad_alloc` exception.
output.beginner: You asked the computer for way more memory than it actually has. It couldn't fulfill the request, so it threw an error.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by dereferencing null pointer.

input.title: Dereferencing Null Pointer

output.technical: A segmentation fault occurs when a program attempts to access a memory location that it is not allowed to access. Dereferencing a `nullptr` attempts to read from memory address 0x0, which is protected by the operating system.
output.beginner: You tried to look inside a box (pointer), but the box didn't exist (it was `nullptr`). The computer panicked and crashed.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

input.title: Stack Overflow (Infinite Recursion)

output.technical: When a function calls itself recursively without a reachable base case, it continuously adds new stack frames to the call stack. Eventually, the stack memory is exhausted, causing a stack overflow (usually manifesting as a segmentation fault).
output.beginner: You told a function to call itself forever without a stopping condition. Eventually, the computer ran out of memory to keep track of the calls, and crashed.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by std::bad_alloc exception.

input.title: std::bad_alloc Exception

output.technical: When the `new` operator fails to allocate the requested amount of contiguous heap memory (either because the system is out of memory or the requested size is absurd), it throws a `std::bad_alloc` exception.
output.beginner: You asked the computer for way more memory than it actually has. It couldn't fulfill the request, so it threw an error.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by dereferencing null pointer.

input.title: Dereferencing Null Pointer

output.technical: A segmentation fault occurs when a program attempts to access a memory location that it is not allowed to access. Dereferencing a `nullptr` attempts to read from memory address 0x0, which is protected by the operating system.
output.beginner: You tried to look inside a box (pointer), but the box didn't exist (it was `nullptr`). The computer panicked and crashed.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by std::out_of_range exception.

input.title: std::out_of_range Exception
input.error_message:
terminate called after throwing an instance of 'std::out_of_range'
  what():  vector::_M_range_check: __n (which is 5) >= this->size() (which is 3)
Aborted (core dumped)

output.technical: The `std::vector::at()` method performs bounds checking. If the requested index is greater than or equal to the vector's size, it throws a `std::out_of_range` exception, which terminates the program if unhandled.
output.beginner: You asked for the 6th item in a list that only has 3 items. C++ caught the mistake and threw an exception to stop you from reading random memory.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

input.title: Dereferencing Null Pointer

output.technical: A segmentation fault occurs when a program attempts to access a memory location that it is not allowed to access. Dereferencing a `nullptr` attempts to read from memory address 0x0, which is protected by the operating system.
output.beginner: You tried to look inside a box (pointer), but the box didn't exist (it was `nullptr`). The computer panicked and crashed.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by std::out_of_range exception.

input.title: std::out_of_range Exception
input.error_message:
terminate called after throwing an instance of 'std::out_of_range'
  what():  vector::_M_range_check: __n (which is 5) >= this->size() (which is 3)
Aborted (core dumped)

output.technical: The `std::vector::at()` method performs bounds checking. If the requested index is greater than or equal to the vector's size, it throws a `std::out_of_range` exception, which terminates the program if unhandled.
output.beginner: You asked for the 6th item in a list that only has 3 items. C++ caught the mistake and threw an exception to stop you from reading random memory.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by integer division by zero.

input.title: Integer Division by Zero

output.technical: Attempting to perform integer division by zero raises a SIGFPE (Floating-Point Exception) signal in POSIX systems, which typically terminates the process immediately.
output.beginner: You tried to divide a number by zero. Just like in math class, this is impossible, so the computer crashes.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by stack overflow (infinite recursion).

input.title: Stack Overflow (Infinite Recursion)

output.technical: When a function calls itself recursively without a reachable base case, it continuously adds new stack frames to the call stack. Eventually, the stack memory is exhausted, causing a stack overflow (usually manifesting as a segmentation fault).
output.beginner: You told a function to call itself forever without a stopping condition. Eventually, the computer ran out of memory to keep track of the calls, and crashed.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by std::bad_alloc exception.

input.title: std::bad_alloc Exception

output.technical: When the `new` operator fails to allocate the requested amount of contiguous heap memory (either because the system is out of memory or the requested size is absurd), it throws a `std::bad_alloc` exception.
output.beginner: You asked the computer for way more memory than it actually has. It couldn't fulfill the request, so it threw an error.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by stack overflow (infinite recursion).

input.title: Stack Overflow (Infinite Recursion)

output.technical: When a function calls itself recursively without a reachable base case, it continuously adds new stack frames to the call stack. Eventually, the stack memory is exhausted, causing a stack overflow (usually manifesting as a segmentation fault).
output.beginner: You told a function to call itself forever without a stopping condition. Eventually, the computer ran out of memory to keep track of the calls, and crashed.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by std::bad_alloc exception.

input.title: std::bad_alloc Exception

output.technical: When the `new` operator fails to allocate the requested amount of contiguous heap memory (either because the system is out of memory or the requested size is absurd), it throws a `std::bad_alloc` exception.
output.beginner: You asked the computer for way more memory than it actually has. It couldn't fulfill the request, so it threw an error.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by dereferencing null pointer.

input.title: Dereferencing Null Pointer

output.technical: A segmentation fault occurs when a program attempts to access a memory location that it is not allowed to access. Dereferencing a `nullptr` attempts to read from memory address 0x0, which is protected by the operating system.
output.beginner: You tried to look inside a box (pointer), but the box didn't exist (it was `nullptr`). The computer panicked and crashed.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by std::out_of_range exception.

input.title: std::out_of_range Exception
input.error_message:
terminate called after throwing an instance of 'std::out_of_range'
  what():  vector::_M_range_check: __n (which is 5) >= this->size() (which is 3)
Aborted (core dumped)

output.technical: The `std::vector::at()` method performs bounds checking. If the requested index is greater than or equal to the vector's size, it throws a `std::out_of_range` exception, which terminates the program if unhandled.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by std::bad_alloc exception.

input.title: std::bad_alloc Exception

output.technical: When the `new` operator fails to allocate the requested amount of contiguous heap memory (either because the system is out of memory or the requested size is absurd), it throws a `std::bad_alloc` exception.
output.beginner: You asked the computer for way more memory than it actually has. It couldn't fulfill the request, so it threw an error.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by dereferencing null pointer.

input.title: Dereferencing Null Pointer

output.technical: A segmentation fault occurs when a program attempts to access a memory location that it is not allowed to access. Dereferencing a `nullptr` attempts to read from memory address 0x0, which is protected by the operating system.
output.beginner: You tried to look inside a box (pointer), but the box didn't exist (it was `nullptr`). The computer panicked and crashed.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by std::out_of_range exception.

input.title: std::out_of_range Exception
input.error_message:
terminate called after throwing an instance of 'std::out_of_range'
  what():  vector::_M_range_check: __n (which is 5) >= this->size() (which is 3)
Aborted (core dumped)

output.technical: The `std::vector::at()` method performs bounds checking. If the requested index is greater than or equal to the vector's size, it throws a `std::out_of_range` exception, which terminates the program if unhandled.
output.beginner: You asked for the 6th item in a list that only has 3 items. C++ caught the mistake and threw an exception to stop you from reading random memory.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by std::out_of_range exception.

input.title: std::out_of_range Exception
input.error_message:
terminate called after throwing an instance of 'std::out_of_range'
  what():  vector::_M_range_check: __n (which is 5) >= this->size() (which is 3)
Aborted (core dumped)

output.technical: The `std::vector::at()` method performs bounds checking. If the requested index is greater than or equal to the vector's size, it throws a `std::out_of_range` exception, which terminates the program if unhandled.
output.beginner: You asked for the 6th item in a list that only has 3 items. C++ caught the mistake and threw an exception to stop you from reading random memory.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by stack overflow (infinite recursion).

input.title: Stack Overflow (Infinite Recursion)

output.technical: When a function calls itself recursively without a reachable base case, it continuously adds new stack frames to the call stack. Eventually, the stack memory is exhausted, causing a stack overflow (usually manifesting as a segmentation fault).
output.beginner: You told a function to call itself forever without a stopping condition. Eventually, the computer ran out of memory to keep track of the calls, and crashed.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.

instruction: Fix the C++ runtime error caused by std::bad_alloc exception.

input.title: std::bad_alloc Exception

output.technical: When the `new` operator fails to allocate the requested amount of contiguous heap memory (either because the system is out of memory or the requested size is absurd), it throws a `std::bad_alloc` exception.
output.beginner: You asked the computer for way more memory than it actually has. It couldn't fulfill the request, so it threw an error.
output.analogy: It's like a machine trying to process an impossible instruction and physically breaking in the attempt.
output.solution: Add appropriate boundary checks, base cases for recursion, or `try-catch` blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.
output.prevention: Always validate inputs, check pointers against `nullptr` before dereferencing, and use smart pointers / STL containers to manage memory safely.
